success_surface: Formula-based conditional aggregation with proper reference handling and multi-method validation
effective_behaviors: Comprehensive structural exploration before implementation to identify data layout and target ranges
Selecting appropriate Excel function based on aggregation requirements (SUMIF for conditional sums vs LOOKUP for single-value retrieval)
Using mixed cell references correctly: absolute references for fixed ranges, relative references for criteria that vary by row
Batch applying formulas across multiple target cells using iteration rather than individual cell assignments
Cross-validating results using both workbook inspection and external data analysis tools
Preserving original source file by saving modified output to new path
Providing comprehensive logging of operations including formulas applied and verification data
evidence: Agent explored complete spreadsheet content including all rows, columns, max dimensions before coding
Agent selected SUMIF instead of SUM+XLOOKUP to properly aggregate across multiple matching rows
Formula used $C$4:$C$11 and $D$4:$D$11 (absolute) with F{row} (relative) enabling correct copy-down behavior
Applied formulas via loop for rows 4-6 instead of hardcoding each cell
Verified formulas via ws[f'G{row}'].value AND calculated expected totals with pandas groupby
Saved to /mnt/data/outputs/... while keeping source file at /mnt/data/spreadsheet/... untouched
Printed formula strings, verification results, and manual data summary for transparency

memory_items.title: Pre-Implementation Structural Analysis
memory_items.lesson: Always inspect complete spreadsheet structure before implementing formulas or data operations
memory_items.generalization: Query sheet names, iterate through all populated rows/columns, determine max dimensions, and examine target cell areas to accurately map data relationships before coding any modifications.

memory_items.title: Function Selection for Aggregation Tasks
memory_items.lesson: Choose SUMIF/SUMIFS over LOOKUP functions when aggregating multiple matching records
memory_items.generalization: For tasks requiring summation/counting across multiple rows matching a criteria, prefer aggregation functions (SUMIF, COUNTIF, SUMPRODUCT) over single-value lookup functions (VLOOKUP, XLOOKUP, INDEX-MATCH).

memory_items.title: Mixed Reference Strategy for Copy-Down Formulas
memory_items.lesson: Use absolute references for static ranges and relative references for per-row criteria in batch-applied formulas
memory_items.generalization: When writing formulas to be applied across multiple rows or columns, lock range references with $ symbols while allowing the lookup/input cell to shift automatically based on position.

memory_items.title: Dual-Method Result Validation
memory_items.lesson: Validate formula results through both direct formula inspection and independent data recalculation
memory_items.generalization: After setting formulas, verify correctness by checking both that formulas are written correctly in the workbook AND that computed outputs match independently calculated expectations using external data processing.

memory_items.title: Batch Formula Application Pattern
memory_items.lesson: Use iteration loops to apply identical formula logic across multiple target cells
memory_items.generalization: Instead of individually assigning formulas to each cell in a range, construct parameterized formula strings and iterate through the target cells to ensure consistency and maintainability.

edits.content:
## 6. Trace-Derived Lessons

### Pre-Implementation Structural Analysis
- Always inspect complete spreadsheet structure before implementing formulas or data operations.
- Query sheet names, iterate through all populated rows/columns, determine max dimensions, and examine target cell areas to accurately map data relationships before coding any modifications.

### Function Selection for Aggregation Tasks
- For tasks requiring summation/counting across multiple rows matching a criteria, prefer aggregation functions (SUMIF, COUNTIF, SUMPRODUCT) over single-value lookup functions (VLOOKUP, XLOOKUP, INDEX-MATCH).

### Mixed Reference Strategy for Copy-Down Formulas
- When writing formulas to be applied across multiple rows or columns, lock range references with $ symbols while allowing the lookup/input cell to shift automatically based on position.

### Dual-Method Result Validation
- After setting formulas, verify correctness by checking both that formulas are written correctly in the workbook AND that computed outputs match independently calculated expectations using external data processing (e.g., pandas).

### Batch Formula Application Pattern
- Instead of individually assigning formulas to each cell in a range, construct parameterized formula strings and iterate through the target cells to ensure consistency and maintainability.
